edibility: Tree pathogen, not edible; causes silverleaf disease in plants. Extremely rare human infection documented in one immunocompromised mycologist; most people have no risk.
taste: Not eaten; texture is thin, leathery, tough, and papery with poor palatability.
search: Found on Prunus species (plums, cherries, peaches, apples) and other Rosaceae family trees; thin purple-tinged or gray-brown brackets with whitish hairy margins that darken with age. Look-alikes include Stereum species, but Chondrostereum has smoother hymenium. Most commonly seen in UK woodlands on decaying hardwood.

toxicity.symptoms: In the single documented human case: hoarseness, cough, recurrent pharyngitis, fatigue, difficulty swallowing, anorexia. Plant pathogen; human infection extremely rare.
toxicity.onset: Unknown; documented case developed symptoms over extended occupational exposure to plant fungi
toxicity.treatmentWindow: Unknown; fungal infections typically require weeks to months of antifungal therapy
toxicity.antidote: Antifungal medications (itraconazole or voriconazole) if systemic infection suspected; seek medical care immediately if respiratory symptoms develop after exposure
